mappings.rationaleAI: Covers Egypt under British rule 1882-1922 (reforms, nationalist revival, WWI independence) matching B.1.b, and the Scramble for Africa, colonial administration in West, East, and Central Africa matching D.1-D.5. The British Empire article addresses both the Egyptian and sub-Saharan colonial dimensions central to this section.

WWI directly caused the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire (A.2.c), and the article covers British encouragement of Arab nationalism and the postwar settlement including the Balfour Declaration and partition of Ottoman territories (B.3.a).

Directly covers the Tanzimat reforms (A.2.a), Mahmud II's reign and centralisation (A.1), the Young Turk Revolution and Abdulhamid II's nationalism (A.2.c), and the empire's WWI participation and dissolution. The Ottoman Empire article is the primary source for the entire A strand of the outline.

Covers Muhammad Ali's centralized administration and successors (B.1.a), the Suez Canal, European financial and military intervention, British occupation 1882-1922, Baring's reforms, nationalist revival, and 1922 independence (B.1.b). Egypt is the primary article for the entire B.1 path.

Napoleon's Egyptian campaign (1798-1801) is explicitly the opening event of outline path B.1.a, marking the beginning of French and then British occupation of Egypt. The article covers this formative episode.
Algeria directly maps to B.2.a, covering the French conquest beginning 1830, the colonial settlement regime, and the national resistance movement under Abdelkader that the outline explicitly names.

Directly covers the 1884 Berlin Conference and European partition of Africa (D.1), colonial administration and African resistance in West Africa (D.2), and the broader process of European conquest across sub-Saharan Africa in the period 1880-1920. This is the central article for the entire D strand.

Explicitly covers Tewodros II (1855-68), Yohannes IV (1872-89), and Menilek II (1889-1913) as well as struggles against Egypt, the Sudan, and Italy — all named in outline path D.3.b on the consolidation of central power in Ethiopia.

Nigeria's pre-colonial Sokoto Caliphate and British colonisation of West Africa directly illustrate D.2 on European colonies in West Africa, particularly the challenges of establishing colonial regimes and reliance on African political structures (D.2.b).

The decline in the slave trade is the first element of outline path D.1, which explicitly precedes the discussion of European commercial, missionary, and exploratory activities as drivers of the Scramble for Africa.

Covers Arab nationalism, the Arab-Israeli conflict origins, and Zionism as factors in Middle Eastern affairs — directly matching B.3.a (Arab nationalism, British encouragement in WWI, postwar settlement) and B.3.b (origins of Zionist movement and conflict with Arab nationalists).

Directly covers Leopold II's Congo Free State, its establishment, and Belgian Congo policies through WWI — the specific content of outline path D.5.b on King Leopold II's colonial enterprise in the Congo.

Turkey's history covers the Abdulhamid II era, the Young Turk Revolution (1908), the Committee of Union and Progress, and the final dissolution of the Ottoman Empire — the core content of A.2.c on the growth of Turkish nationalism.

Russian intrusions into Turkey are explicitly named in A.1 (empire under Mahmud II), and Russian penetration of Iran by competition with Britain is the central theme of C.1 on Iran under the Qajar dynasty. Russia's imperial expansion in both directions is core to understanding this section.

Addresses the theoretical and practical frameworks of colonial rule — mercantilism, chartered companies, coercive institutions — applicable to D.1 (imperialistic scramble and European partition of Africa) and D.2 (establishment of colonial regimes in West Africa).

New Imperialism drove the Scramble for Africa (D.1) and European penetration of the Maghrib (B.2); the article covers Social Darwinism and civilising mission justifications and Lenin's and Hobson's economic theories that explain the 1880-1920 colonial expansion this section describes.

Covers European colonial penetration from the 15th century through anti-colonial movements, with particular attention to the Scramble for Africa period. Provides broad contextual coverage for the entire D strand of this section.

Italy's Libyan conquest (1911-12) is the specific event that closes outline path B.2.d on Libya (Tripolitania and Cyrenaica) from c.1834 to 1920, including subjection to direct Turkish rule and the Italian conquest.
